en.extract:
The white-headed marsh tyrant (Arundinicola leucocephala), commonly referred to as the marsh tyrant, is a small passerine bird belonging to the tyrant flycatcher family. It is the sole species within the genus Arundinicola. This diurnal bird, exclusively found in South America, exhibits sexual dimorphism and is known to have monogamous mating behavior.


Description


Males
Male marsh tyrants possess a distinct appearance, featuring a white head with a small crest, a white throat, and a contrasting black body. They have dark brown irises, black legs, and a bill that consists of a black upper mandible and a yellow lower mandible. Typically, they weigh around 13 grams and measure approximately 13 centimeters in length.


Females
Female marsh tyrants have a white forecrown and throat, along with a greyish-brown breast and flanks. Their wings and tail are also greyish-brown, although they are more uniform and significantly darker. The average weight of a female ranges from 12 to 12.5 grams, and she generally measures 13 centimeters in length. Females and juveniles have similar appearances.


Taxonomy
The white-headed marsh tyrant is a monotypic species within the Tyrannidae family, classified under the Fluvicolinae subfamily, which splits into two tribes: Contopini (peewees, phoebes, and allies) and Fluvicolini (ground-tyrants and allies). A. leucocephala, along with 71 other species, belongs to the Fluvicolini tribe. Additionally, it forms a clade with the Black-backed water-tyrant (Fluvicola albiventer), the Pied water-tyrant (Fluvicola pica), and the Masked water-tyrant (Fluvicola nengeta) due to resembling internal anatomical characteristics. Their similar plumages, nesting behaviors, and link to stagnant waters further emphasize their tight connection.
The Latin name of the white-headed marsh tyrant reflects both its habitat and distinctive appearance. The term "Arundo" translates to "reed", while "nicola" means "dweller". Furthermore, "leucocephala"  signifies "white head".


Habitat and distribution
The species is native to several South American countries, including Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Paraguay, Peru, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, and Venezuela.
As its name suggests, these tyrants inhabit wetlands in tropical regions, including marshes, bogs, swamps, fens, peatlands, rivers, streams, creeks, and moist savannas. Additionally, many of the previously mentioned countries, such as Brazil, Colombia, Peru, Ecuador, and Paraguay, dedicate a substantial amount of land to rice production. A. leucocephala has been reported as one of the many non-aquatic species found in these ricelands.
A. leucocephala is a resident bird, but it may travel based on rapid turnover and succession in different environments if suitable conditions arise. Some relocated marsh tyrants have been documented from northeastern Peru and northeastern Ecuador along the Napo River.


Behavior


Vocalizations

They are usually quiet, but they do emit a high-pitched call "sedik!". During courtship, they produce a deeper call "dew-de-lewde" , which is repeated at short intervals.


Diet
A. leucocephala is an insectivores, primarily preying on flying insects including dragonflies, grasshoppers, froghoppers and beetles.


Foraging
Tyrant flycatchers use many different foraging techniques, such as the sally strike, sally glide, sally hover, sally pounce, leap upward, and leap downward. The choice of technique is often associated with differences in bill, wing, and tarsus structure. The sally strike – attacking in a fluid movement without gliding, hovering, or landing — is the most frequently used foraging maneuver by the white-headed marsh tyrant.

A. leucocephala captures most of its prey at the water's surface. Its next most commonly used foraging substrate is in the air, followed by live leaves. They tend to perch low on small marsh plants (less than 2 meters high) and attack their prey at a distance of 3 to 4 meters from their initial position. They typically target prey that is at or below their height while perched, and they less frequently attack at a high flight angle. Once they catch their prey, they promptly return to their perch, as they are rarely found on the ground.
Both female and male marsh tyrants participate in feeding their juveniles. The main differences are that the female marsh tyrant hunts at greater distances and makes more frequent visits to the young compared to the male. Moreover, the female is more active in maintaining a sanitary environment for the nestlings. The parents – primarily the female – remove arthropod remains and fecal sacs from the nest, while the juveniles contribute to the cleaning by handing the arthropod remains to their parents. It is theorized that keeping the nest clean helps reduce the risk of infestation by parasites and pathogens.
It is speculated that if the male were to be more active, it could increase the nest's vulnerability to predation and may even increase the risk of predation on the male himself by nearby raptors. This is largely due to the more prominent colors of the male tyrant, which make him more noticeable in his habitat compared to the female.


Reproduction

Nests are typically constructed within four feet of the ground in swamp bushes. They are spherical in shape with a roof and feature a side opening near the top known as the "porch".  This opening is narrow, to help protect the nest against predation. The nests are mainly made of dried grass, with the chambers lined thickly with soft materials like down. These homes measure about 4 inches high and 3 inches wide. The tyrants do not attempt to conceal their nests, making them quite noticeable. Both parents participate in their construction.
In Satarem, Brazil, many nests were found within inches of the hive of a large species wasp. Consequently, the natives called the marsh tyrant "Mother of the Wasp".
In order of frequency, the most popular breeding months are September, June, October and August; followed by November and July. The eggs laid are a creamy white color, with the number of eggs in a single brood ranging from 1 to 3. Each egg weighs between 1.8 and 1.9 grams and measures approximately 19.20 to 20.68 millimeters in length and 11.10 to 14.58 millimeters in width. The incubation period lasts about 12 to 16 days and the task is shared by both parents. The hatchlings remain in the nest for about 15–17 days before they are ready to leave. The generation length of the marsh tyrants is around 3.6 years.

The marsh tyrant is often found in pairs or in a small family group; nevertheless it does sometimes forage alone. The bird exhibits evidence of monogamy, as most individuals spend the majority of their time in couples. However, once one of the partners in the duo disappears, it is replaced by another. To attract the opposite sex, the marsh tyrant performs pre-nuptial flights. During courtship, the crest is prominent and can be bushy or flattened, depending on the moment. Usually, this performance includes an erect crest, high vocalizations, and various wing positions (raised, fanned or fluttering). This behavior is also employed when confronting rivals in aggressive disputes. The tyrant rarely spreads or cocks its tail.


Mate aggression
A. leucocephala displays mate aggression between breeding pairs, a behavior observed only during the breeding season. In these couples, the males are more aggressive; when they hear the call of a male intruder, they direct their aggression toward their paired female instead of the intruder. When perched, the male opens and flutters its wings while slightly opening its beak. Then, the male aggressively flies over and chases its mate. In response, the female assumes a defensive posture, opening her beak and partially spreading her wings. To evade the male, she may move down the branch or attempt to fly away. This combative conduct can lead to physical harm, increased stress, drained energy, and reduced reproductive success.


Threats


Shiny cowbirds (Molthrus bonariensis)
The shiny cowbird, found in South America, Panama, and the West Indies, is an obligate brood parasite that targets 176 different bird species as its hosts. In many areas like Guyana and Trinidad, A. leucocephala specifically is known to be a frequent host choice for M. bonariensis. When the brood parasite inhabits wetlands, it tends to prey upon the white-headed marsh tyrant and the Pied water tyrant .


Human activity
In Brazil's Atlantic Forest, which is home to the marsh tyrant, deforestation poses a significant threat to many plant and animal species. The growing human population endangers wildlife habitats that lack protection from reserves, national parks, or conservation groups. However, even these protected areas are not immune to exploitation, as they are often targeted for their resources (timber, orchids, etc.) as well as for hunting and bird trapping


Status and conservation
This bird is considered fairly common to locally common and is not deemed threatened by the IUCN. Its habitat is located in many national parks and other protected areas which help preserve the environment.
Regions of northeastern Argentina (2%), eastern Paraguay (6%), and eastern Brazil (92%) are part of the Atlantic Forest of South America, which is one of the world's essential biodiversity strongholds.  Within this forest, many nature conservation areas are home to the white-headed marsh tyrant, thereby protecting both the bird and its habitat. The following nature reserves house the tyrant: Tapyta Private Nature Reserve, Ypeti Nature Reserve, and Mbaracayú Binational.


References


External links
Arundinicola leucocephala songs on Xeno-canto
White-headed marsh tyrants images – Wikimedia Commons
White-headed Marsh Tyrant – Birds of the World
White-headed Marsh Tyrant images from the Macaulay Library and Cornell Lab of Ornithology
BirdLife species factsheet White-headed Marsh-tyrant
White-headed Marsh-Tyrant – Avibase – The World Bird Database

The '''white-headed marsh tyrant''' ('''''Arundinicola leucocephala'''''), commonly referred to as the marsh tyrant, is a small [[passerine]] bird belonging to the [[tyrant flycatcher]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]]. It is the sole [[species]] within the [[genus]] ''Arundinicola''. This [[Diurnality|diurnal]] bird, exclusively found in South America, exhibits [[sexual dimorphism]] and is known to have [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] mating behavior.

== Description ==

=== Males ===
Male marsh tyrants possess a distinct appearance, featuring a white head with a small crest, a white throat, and a contrasting black body. They have dark brown irises, black legs, and a bill that consists of a black upper mandible and a yellow lower mandible. Typically, they weigh around 13 grams and measure approximately 13 centimeters in length.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Ewing |first=Nikita |date=2017 |title=Arundinicola leucocephala (White-headed Marsh Tyrant) |url=https://sta.uwi.edu/fst/lifesciences/sites/default/files/lifesciences/images/Arundinicola%20leucocephala%20-%20White-headed%20Marsh%20Tyrant.pdf |access-date=28 September 2024 |website=The Online Guide to the Animals of Trinidad and Tobago}}</ref>

=== Females ===
Female marsh tyrants have a white forecrown and throat, along with a greyish-brown breast and flanks. Their wings and tail are also greyish-brown, although they are more uniform and significantly darker. The average weight of a female ranges from 12 to 12.5 grams, and she generally measures 13 centimeters in length. Females and juveniles have similar appearances.<ref name=":0" />

== Taxonomy ==
The white-headed marsh tyrant is a [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] species within the ''[[Tyrant flycatcher|Tyrannidae]]'' family, classified under the ''[[Fluvicolinae]]'' subfamily, which splits into two tribes: ''Contopini'' (peewees, phoebes, and allies) and ''Fluvicolini'' (ground-tyrants and allies). ''A. leucocephala, along'' with 71 other species, belongs to the ''Fluvicolini'' tribe.<ref name=":6">{{Cite book |last1=Hoyo |first1=del Josep |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World |last2=Elliott |first2=Andrew |last3=Christie |first3=David |date=2004 |publisher=Lynx Edicions |isbn=84-87334-69-5 |volume=9. Cotingas to Pipits and Wagtails |location=Barcelona}}</ref> Additionally, it forms a clade with the [[Black-backed water tyrant|Black-backed water-tyrant]] (''Fluvicola albiventer''), the [[Pied water tyrant|Pied water-tyrant]] (''Fluvicola pica''), and the [[Masked water tyrant|Masked water-tyrant]] (''Fluvicola nengeta'') due to resembling internal anatomical characteristics. Their similar plumages, nesting behaviors, and link to stagnant waters further emphasize their tight connection.<ref name=":6" />

The [[Latin]] name of the white-headed marsh tyrant reflects both its [[habitat]] and distinctive appearance. The term "''Arundo''" translates to "reed", while "''nicola''" means "dweller". Furthermore, "''leucocephala''"  signifies "white head".

== Habitat and distribution ==
The species is native to several [[List of sovereign states and dependent territories in South America|South American countries]], including [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], Brazil, [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], French Guiana, [[Paraguay]], [[Peru]], [[Suriname]], [[Trinidad and Tobago]], and [[Venezuela]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=White-headed Marsh-tyrant (Arundinicola leucocephala) – BirdLife species factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/white-headed-marsh-tyrant-arundinicola-leucocephala/details |access-date=2 October 2024 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref>

As its name suggests, these tyrants inhabit [[wetland]]s in tropical regions, including [[marsh]]es, [[bog]]s, [[swamp]]s, fens, [[peatland]]s, rivers, streams, creeks, and moist savannas.<ref name=":1" /> Additionally, many of the previously mentioned countries, such as Brazil, [[Colombia]], [[Peru]], [[Ecuador]], and [[Paraguay]], dedicate a substantial amount of land to rice production. ''A. leucocephala'' has been reported as one of the many non-aquatic species found in these ricelands.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Acosta |first1=Martín |last2=Mugica |first2=Lourdes |last3=Blanco |first3=Daniel |last4=López-Lanús |first4=Bernabé |last5=Dias |first5=Rafael Antunes |last6=Doodnath |first6=Lester W. |last7=Hurtado |first7=Joanna |date=2010 |title=Birds of Rice Fields in the Americas |journal=Waterbirds: The International Journal of Waterbird Biology |volume=33 |pages=105–122 |doi=10.1675/063.033.s108 |jstor=40891070 |issn=1524-4695}}</ref>

''A. leucocephala'' is a resident bird, but it may travel based on rapid turnover and succession in different environments if suitable conditions arise. Some relocated marsh tyrants have been documented from northeastern [[Peru]] and northeastern [[Ecuador]] along the [[Napo River]].<ref name=":9" />

== Behavior ==
=== Vocalizations ===
{{Birdsong|species = ''White-headed Marsh Tyrant ''|url = https://xeno-canto.org/species/Arundinicola-leucocephala |song1 = |caption song1 = |image = }}
They are usually quiet, but they do emit a high-pitched call "sedik!". During courtship, they produce a deeper call "dew-de-lewde" , which is repeated at short intervals.<ref name=":9">{{Cite web |last1=Farnsworth |first1=Andrew |last2=Langham |first2=Gary |date=4 March 2020 |title=White-headed Marsh Tyrant |url=https://birdsoftheworld-org.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/bow/species/whmtyr1/cur/introduction |access-date=28 September 2024 |website=Birds of the World}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
''A. leucocephala'' is an [[insectivore]]s, primarily preying on flying insects including [[dragonflies]], [[grasshopper]]s, [[froghopper]]s and [[beetle]]s.<ref name=":0" />[[File:Arundinicola leucocephala (Monjita pantanera) (14047879697).jpg|thumb|White-headed marsh tyrant vocalizing.]]

=== Foraging ===
[[Tyrant flycatcher]]s use many different foraging techniques, such as the sally strike, sally glide, sally hover, sally pounce, leap upward, and leap downward. The choice of technique is often associated with differences in bill, [[wing]], and tarsus structure. The sally strike – attacking in a fluid movement without gliding, hovering, or landing<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Remsen |first1=J.V |last2=Robinson |first2=Jr. |last3=Robinson |first3=Scott K. |date=1990 |title=A classification scheme for foraging behavior of birds in terrestrial habitats |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/SAB_013_1988%20P144-160_A%20Classification%20Scheme%20for%20Foraging%20Behavior%20of%20Birds%20in%20Terrestrial%20Habitats_J.%20V.%20Remsen%2C%20Scott%20K.%20Robinson.pdf |journal=Studies in Avian Biology |issue=13 |pages=144–160}}</ref> — is the most frequently used foraging maneuver by the white-headed marsh tyrant.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Gabriel |first1=Vagner de A. |last2=Pizo |first2=Marco A. |date=December 2005 |title=Foraging behavior of tyrant flycatchers (Aves, Tyrannidae) in Brazil |url=https://www.scielo.br/j/rbzool/a/CVvYjTjNnQyBrxzpbhgw83r/?lang=en |journal=Revista Brasileira de Zoologia |language=en |volume=22 |issue=4 |pages=1072–1077 |doi=10.1590/S0101-81752005000400036 |issn=0101-8175|hdl=11449/21133 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
[[File:Lavadeira-de-cabeça-branca (Arundinicola leucocephala) é uma ave passeriforme da família dos tiranídeos, com ampla distribuição no Brasil e países adjacentes, em pântanos e lagoas. - panoramio.jpg|thumb|Perching on a small marsh plant.]]
''A. leucocephala'' captures most of its prey at the water's surface. Its next most commonly used foraging substrate is in the [[Atmosphere of Earth|air]], followed by live [[Leaf|leaves]]. They tend to perch low on small marsh plants (less than 2 meters high) and attack their [[Predation|prey]] at a distance of 3 to 4 meters from their initial position. They typically target [[Predation|prey]] that is at or below their height while perched, and they less frequently attack at a high flight angle.<ref name=":2" /> Once they catch their [[Predation|prey]], they promptly return to their perch, as they are rarely found on the ground.<ref name=":5">{{Cite web |last=Bouglouan |first=Nicole |title=White-headed Marsh-Tyrant |url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-white-headed-marsh-tyrant.html#:~:text=White%2Dheaded%20Marsh%2DTyrant's%20nest,Tyrant%20feeds%20mainly%20on%20insects. |access-date=7 October 2024 |website=Oiseaux-birds.com}}</ref>

Both female and male marsh tyrants participate in feeding their juveniles. The main differences are that the female marsh tyrant hunts at greater distances and makes more frequent visits to the young compared to the male. Moreover, the female is more active in maintaining a sanitary environment for the nestlings. The parents – primarily the female – remove [[arthropod]] remains and [[fecal sac]]s from the nest, while the juveniles contribute to the cleaning by handing the [[arthropod]] remains to their parents. It is theorized that keeping the nest clean helps reduce the risk of infestation by parasites and pathogens.<ref name=":0" />

It is speculated that if the male were to be more active, it could increase the nest's vulnerability to [[predation]] and may even increase the risk of [[predation]] on the male himself by nearby [[Bird of prey|raptors]]. This is largely due to the more prominent colors of the male tyrant, which make him more noticeable in his [[habitat]] compared to the female.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last=Cestari |first=César |date=December 2023 |title=Sexual Differences in Parental Feeding Effort during the Nestling Period of the White-headed Marsh-tyrant (arundinicola Leucocephala) |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2077&context=ornitologia_neotropical |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical |volume=23 |issue=2}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===
[[File:Abbildungen zur Naturgeschichte Brasiliens (Plate 31) (6011862809).jpg|thumb|A globular nest.]]
[[Nest]]s are typically constructed within four feet of the ground in swamp bushes. They are [[Sphere|spherical]] in shape with a roof and feature a side opening near the top known as the "porch".  This opening is narrow, to help protect the nest against [[predation]]. The [[nest]]s are mainly made of dried [[Poaceae|grass]], with the chambers lined thickly with soft materials like down. These homes measure about 4 inches high and 3 inches wide. The tyrants do not attempt to conceal their [[nest]]s, making them quite noticeable.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Lamm |first=Donald W. |date=1948 |title=Notes on the Birds of the States of Pernambuco and Paraiba, Brazil |journal=The Auk |volume=65 |issue=2 |pages=261–283 |doi=10.2307/4080302 |jstor=4080302 |issn=0004-8038}}</ref> Both parents participate in their construction.<ref name=":0" />

In [[Santarém, Pará|Satarem, Brazil]], many [[nest]]s were found within inches of the hive of a large species [[wasp]]. Consequently, the natives called the marsh tyrant "Mother of the Wasp".<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Riker |first1=Clarence B. |last2=Chapman |first2=Frank M. |date=1890 |title=A List of Birds Observed at Santarem, Brazil (Continued) |journal=The Auk |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=265–271 |doi=10.2307/4067986 |jstor=4067986 |issn=0004-8038}}</ref>

In order of frequency, the most popular breeding months are September, June, October and August; followed by November and July.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Cruz |first1=Alexander |last2=Andrews |first2=Robert W. |date=1989 |title=Observations on the Breeding Biology of Passerines in a Seasonally Flooded Savanna in Venezuela |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=101 |issue=1 |pages=62–76 |jstor=4162688 |issn=0043-5643}}</ref> The [[Bird egg|eggs]] laid are a creamy white color, with the number of eggs in a single brood ranging from 1 to 3. Each egg weighs between 1.8 and 1.9 grams and measures approximately 19.20 to 20.68 millimeters in length and 11.10 to 14.58 millimeters in width.<ref name=":4" /> The incubation period lasts about 12 to 16 days and the task is shared by both parents.<ref name=":5" /> The [[hatchling]]s remain in the [[nest]] for about 15–17 days before they are ready to leave. The generation length of the marsh tyrants is around 3.6 years.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web |date=2016 |title=White-headed Marsh-tyrant |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22700291/93767511 |access-date=28 September 2024 |website=IUCN RedList}}</ref>
[[File:Freirinha (Arundinicola leucocephala) - White-headed Marsh Tyrant.jpg|thumb|A pair in Serra, Espírito Santo-Southeast of Brazil.]]
The marsh tyrant is often found in pairs or in a small family group; nevertheless it does sometimes forage alone.<ref name=":5" /> The bird exhibits evidence of [[Monogamy in animals|monogamy]], as most individuals spend the majority of their time in couples. However, once one of the partners in the duo disappears, it is replaced by another. To attract the opposite sex, the marsh tyrant performs pre-nuptial flights.<ref name=":3" /> During courtship, the crest is prominent and can be bushy or flattened, depending on the moment. Usually, this performance includes an erect crest, high vocalizations, and various [[wing]] positions (raised, fanned or fluttering). This behavior is also employed when confronting rivals in aggressive disputes.<ref name=":5" /> The tyrant rarely spreads or cocks its tail.<ref name=":6" />

=== Mate aggression ===
''A. leucocephala'' displays mate aggression between breeding pairs, a behavior observed only during the breeding season. In these couples, the males are more aggressive; when they hear the call of a male intruder, they direct their aggression toward their paired female instead of the intruder.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal |last1=Hoffmann |first1=Diego |last2=Fabres |first2=Brener |last3=de Araújo-Hoffmann |first3=Francielle Paulina |date=July 2024 |title=Angry birds: Mate aggression in Arundinicola leucocephala males triggered by potential invaders |url=https://esajournals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/ecy.4332 |journal=Ecology |language=en |volume=105 |issue=7 |article-number=e4332 |doi=10.1002/ecy.4332 |pmid=38828595 |bibcode=2024Ecol..105E4332H |issn=0012-9658|url-access=subscription }}</ref> When perched, the male opens and flutters its [[wing]]s while slightly opening its [[beak]]. Then, the male aggressively flies over and chases its mate. In response, the female assumes a defensive posture, opening her [[beak]] and partially spreading her [[wing]]s. To evade the male, she may move down the [[branch]] or attempt to fly away. This combative conduct can lead to physical harm, increased stress, drained energy, and reduced reproductive success.<ref name=":8" />

== Threats ==

=== Shiny cowbirds (''Molthrus bonariensis'') ===
The [[shiny cowbird]], found in South America, [[Panama]], and the [[West Indies]], is an obligate [[Brood parasitism|brood parasite]] that targets 176 different bird [[species]] as its hosts.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mason |first=Paul |date=1 January 1986 |title=Brood Parasitism in a Host Generalist, the Shiny Cowbird: 1. The Quality of Different Species as Hosts |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/103/1/52/5191591?login=false |journal=The Auk |volume=103 |issue=1 |pages=52–60 |doi=10.1093/auk/103.1.52 |via=American Ornithological Society}}</ref> In many areas like [[Guyana]] and [[Trinidad]], ''A. leucocephala'' specifically is known to be a frequent host choice for ''[[Shiny cowbird|M. bonariensis]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Friedmann |first1=Herbert |last2=Kiff |first2=Lloyd F. |last3=Rothstein |first3=Stephen I. |date=1977 |title=Further Contribution to Knowledge of the Host Relations of the Parasitic Cowbirds |url=https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/GOVPUB-SI-PURL-gpo36307/pdf/GOVPUB-SI-PURL-gpo36307.pdf |journal=Smithsonian Institution Press |volume=235 |page=54 |via=GovInfo}}</ref> When the [[Brood parasitism|brood parasite]] inhabits [[wetland]]s, it tends to prey upon the white-headed marsh tyrant and the [[Pied water tyrant]] .<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Haverschmidt |first=F |date=1 July 1965 |title=Molothrus bonariensis Parasitizing Fluvicola pica and Arundinicola leucocephala in Surinam |url=https://academic-oup-com.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/auk/article/82/3/508/5197604?searchresult=1 |journal=The Auk |volume=82 |issue=3 |pages=508–509 |doi=10.2307/4083136 |jstor=4083136 |via=American Ornithological Society}}</ref>

=== Human activity ===
In [[Atlantic Forest|Brazil's Atlantic Forest]], which is home to the marsh tyrant, [[deforestation]] poses a significant threat to many plant and animal species. The growing human population endangers [[wildlife]] [[habitat]]s that lack protection from reserves, [[national park]]s, or conservation groups. However, even these protected areas are not immune to exploitation, as they are often targeted for their resources (timber, [[orchid]]s, etc.) as well as for hunting and [[bird trapping]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Parker |first1=Theodore A. |last2=Goerck |first2=Jaqueline M. |date=1997 |title=The Importance of National Parks and Biological Reserves to Bird Conservation in the Atlantic Forest Region of Brazil |journal=Ornithological Monographs |issue=48 |pages=527–541 |doi=10.2307/40157550 |jstor=40157550 |issn=0078-6594}}</ref>

== Status and conservation ==
This bird is considered fairly common to locally common and is not deemed threatened by the [[IUCN]].<ref name=":7" /> Its [[habitat]] is located in many [[national park]]s and other protected areas which help preserve the environment.<ref name=":6" />

Regions of northeastern [[Argentina]] (2%), eastern [[Paraguay]] (6%), and eastern Brazil (92%) are part of the [[Atlantic Forest|Atlantic Forest of South America]], which is one of the world's essential biodiversity strongholds.<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2017 |title=State of the Atlantic Forest: Three countries, 148 million people, one of the richest forests on Earth |url=https://awsassets.panda.org/downloads/documento_fvs_baja.pdf |journal=Fundación Vida Silvestre Argentina & WWF |volume=Puerto Iguazú, Argentina.: Technical Report |page=146 }}</ref>  Within this forest, many nature conservation areas are home to the white-headed marsh tyrant, thereby protecting both the bird and its [[habitat]]. The following nature reserves house the tyrant: Tapyta Private Nature Reserve, Ypeti Nature Reserve, and Mbaracayú Binational.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Esquivel |first1=Alberto |last2=Zarza |first2=Rebecca |last3=Tiffer-Sotomayor |first3=Ruth |last4=Diaz |first4=Alejandrino |last5=Perez |first5=Dario |last6=Velazquez |first6=Myriam |date=2019 |title=Conservation Status and Challenges of the Atlantic Forest Birds of Paraguay |url=https://www.proquest.com/docview/2548368890 |journal=Diversity |volume=11 |issue=12 |page=247 |doi=10.3390/d11120247 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2019Diver..11..247E |id={{ProQuest|2548368890}} }}</ref>

zh.extract:
白头沼泽霸鹟（学名：Arundinicola leucocephala），是雀形目霸鹟科的一种鸟类，属于单型的白头沼泽霸鹟属（学名：Arundinicola）。


特征
白头沼泽霸鹟体重约13.8克，翼长约60.1毫米，喙宽度约5.8毫米，喙厚度约5毫米，嘴峰长约17毫米，跗蹠长约16.9毫米，尾长约46.9毫米。
白头沼泽霸鹟是留鸟，栖息在湖泊、沼泽等湿地环境，它们是食肉动物，主要以昆虫、蜘蛛等陆生无脊椎动物为食。它们分布在哥伦比亚到圭亚那、巴西南部和阿根廷北部、特立尼达岛。
